one_line_takeaway: This SoK paper is a literature synthesis plus original CIFAR-10 reproduction study showing that NTGA, the first black-box clean-label generalization (data-poisoning) attack, is largely defeated by PGD adversarial training (83.41% vs. ~85% clean baseline) and is highly linearly separable at the image level (99.79% logistic-regression accuracy vs. 48.04% for clean data), while several newer methods (Error-minimizing, REM, One-Pixel Shortcut) outperform it on data protection.
key_finding: (1) Table 2 (ResNet50/CIFAR-10, no adversarial training): NTGA yields 55.32% test accuracy versus DeepConfuse 40.21%, Error-minimizing 30.76%, Synthetic 47.30%, Robust Error-Minimizing 52.88%, One-Pixel Shortcut 32.21% (all lower/better protection than NTGA), while Error-maximizing 76.56% and Autoregressive 57.95% are higher (worse protection than NTGA) -- so NTGA beats error-maximizing and autoregressive attacks but is beaten by the other five. (2) Table 3 (VGG19 with PGD adversarial training, radius 4/255): a clean, unprotected dataset yields ~85% test accuracy; NTGA-protected data still yields 83.41% test accuracy under adversarial training (i.e., essentially the same as clean data), as do DeepConfuse (84.91%), Error-maximizing (85.11%), Synthetic (85.84%), and Autoregressive (79.94%) -- demonstrating that adversarial training defeats NTGA's data protection almost entirely. Only Error-minimizing (72.41%), Robust Error-Minimizing (51.09%), and especially One-Pixel Shortcut (12.20%) remain effective under adversarial training. (3) Table 4 (logistic regression on perturbed CIFAR-10 images, training accuracy as proxy for linear separability): clean data reaches only 48.04% training accuracy, but NTGA-perturbed images reach 99.79%, DeepConfuse 98.99%, Error-minimizing 99.98%, One-Pixel Shortcut 100%, and Synthetic 100% -- confirming these are strongly linearly separable at the image level (not just at the perturbation level as prior work showed), while Error-maximizing (76.12%) and REM (77.82%) show only moderate separability and Autoregressive (48.75%) shows essentially none, matching clean data. The paper concludes NTGA's high linear separability is a detectable/exploitable weakness. (4) Citing Sadasivan et al., the paper reports NTGA takes 5.2 hours to generate perturbations versus ~0.5 hours for error-maximizing attacks under the same settings, a generation-cost weakness attributed to NTGA's need to train a surrogate model.
core_contribution: This is a Systematization of Knowledge (SoK) paper, not a novel-attack paper. Its own stated contributions (from the Introduction/Contributions list) are: (1) situating the Neural Tangent Generalization Attack (NTGA) within a taxonomy of attacks against DNNs (data poisoning vs. adversarial, generalization vs. integrity, clean-label vs. dirty-label, black/gray/white-box), showing NTGA is the only clean-label, black-box generalization (availability) attack in the literature; (2) synthesizing seven years of follow-on studies about NTGA that were never assembled by the original authors (Yuan & Wu, 2018/2021) and running the authors' OWN new experiments to verify or adjudicate conflicting claims from that literature -- specifically (a) a ResNet50/CIFAR-10 comparison of NTGA against 7 other data-protection methods (Table 2), (b) an 8-way VGG19 adversarial-training robustness comparison (Table 3, Fig. 4), and (c) a logistic-regression linear-separability test on perturbed CIFAR-10 images across 8 attacks (Table 4); and (3) proposing six concrete future-research directions for NTGA (transferability testing, real-world data, distributed/federated learning, unsupervised/contrastive learning, ensemble learning, and non-image data types).
threat_model: The subject attack (NTGA) is a training-time, clean-label, black-box data-poisoning attack under the 'data protection' / unlearnable-examples framing: a data owner adds imperceptible bounded perturbations delta (||delta||_p <= epsilon) to their own data X_D before releasing it, without changing labels Y_D. An unauthorized downstream party trains an arbitrary, unknown DNN f on the perturbed data; the perturbation is crafted (via the bi-level optimization in Eq. 2, approximated using Neural Tangent Kernels and a Gaussian-process mean function so no target-model weights are needed) to maximize the validation/test loss of whatever model f is eventually trained, i.e., the attacker/defender does not know f in advance (true black-box, in contrast to prior clean-label generalization attacks that assume a fixed surrogate model). The paper's own review-and-experiment contribution does not introduce a new threat model; it evaluates NTGA's poisoned data against three adversary/analyst capabilities studied in the literature and reproduced here: (i) an adversarially-trained downstream model (PGD-AT, perturbation radius 4/255, step size 0.8/255) attempting to train through the poison; (ii) an analyst with access only to the perturbed (not clean) images attempting to detect/exploit linear separability via a logistic-regression classifier; (iii) benign comparison against seven competing data-protection methods (DeepConfuse, error-minimizing, error-maximizing, Synthetic, Robust Error-Minimizing/REM, One-Pixel Shortcut, Autoregressive) under the same pipeline.
limitations: Explicitly self-identified: the reproduction experiments use only the CIFAR-10 benchmark dataset (not ImageNet or MNIST, despite citing that NTGA/competitors have also been evaluated there in prior work), so findings may not generalize across datasets or architectures -- the paper itself cautions that its Table 2 conclusions are 'highly dependent on the dataset and model architecture' and that 'a comprehensive analysis is necessary to identify their performance under different circumstances.' The linear-separability and adversarial-training experiments were likewise run on CIFAR-10 only. As a survey/SoK, most of the claims about NTGA's behavior (vulnerability to JPEG compression, grayscale transformation, diffusion-based removal via AVATAR, failure against unsupervised/contrastive learning such as SimCLR, ineffectiveness of ensemble-learning attacks) are drawn from cited external studies rather than the paper's own experiments, and the paper is explicit about which findings are its own (Tables 2-4, Fig. 4) versus which are reported from Fu et al., Sadasivan et al., Tao et al., Liu et al., Dolatabadi et al., Yu et al., Sandoval-Segura et al., Zhu et al., Hapuarachchi et al. (a self-citation, [37]), Wang et al., and He et al. No new defense or improved attack variant is proposed or evaluated -- Section 4's 'future research insights' are proposed directions, not implemented/tested methods.

datasets_used: CIFAR-10 (all three of the paper's own reproduction experiments: Table 2, Table 3/Fig.4, Table 4); benchmark datasets referenced from cited prior work but not used in this paper's own experiments: ImageNet, MNIST, WebFace, VGGFace2
quantitative_results: Table 2 (ResNet50/CIFAR-10, no adv. training) test accuracy: NTGA 55.32%, DeepConfuse 40.21%, Error-minimizing 30.76%, Error-maximizing 76.56%, Synthetic 47.30%, Robust Error-Minimizing 52.88%, One-Pixel Shortcut 32.21%, Autoregressive 57.95%

Table 3 (VGG19 + PGD adversarial training, radius 4/255) test accuracy: clean baseline ~85%, NTGA 83.41%, DeepConfuse 84.91%, Error-minimizing 72.41%, Error-maximizing 85.11%, Synthetic 85.84%, Robust Error-Minimizing 51.09%, One-Pixel Shortcut 12.20%, Autoregressive 79.94%

Table 4 (logistic-regression training accuracy on perturbed images, linear-separability proxy): Clean 48.04%, NTGA 99.79%, DeepConfuse 98.99%, Error-minimizing 99.98%, Error-maximizing 76.12%, Synthetic 100%, Robust Error-Minimizing 77.82%, One-Pixel Shortcut 100%, Autoregressive 48.75%
NTGA-perturbation generation time (cited from Sadasivan et al., not reproduced by this paper): 5.2 hours vs. ~0.5 hours for error-maximizing attack under the same settings
Cited external result: Sandoval-Segura et al. improved ResNet18/CIFAR-10 test accuracy on NTGA-perturbed data from 40.78% to 82.21% using linear transformation removal of perturbations
baselines_compared: One-Pixel Shortcut (Wu et al./Yu et al. cite [95])
